proof:
We prove that the sequence satisfies a linear recurrence of the form \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for all \(n\) with positive integers \(T,L\).  The key idea is to analyse the smallest prime factor of the terms and to show that the set of primes that ever appear is actually finite (or that the sequence becomes periodic with a fixed modulus).  The construction is based on the following observations.

Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  Then \(a_2 = a_1 + p\) (because \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\) and the next multiple of \(p\) is \(a_1+p\); any smaller number would have to be a multiple of some other prime divisor \(q>p\) but the distance to the next multiple of \(q\) is \(q-p\ge p\), so it is not smaller).  Thus \(a_2\) is even if \(p\) is odd, so \(2\) appears as a divisor of \(a_2\).

Now consider the other prime divisors of \(a_1\).  For each such prime \(q\) (with \(q>p\)) the next multiple of \(q\) after \(a_2\) is at distance \(q-p\) from \(a_2\).  If there exists a prime \(q\) with \(q-p<p\) then the smallest integer \(>a_2\) that shares a factor with both \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) is \(a_2+(q-p)\) (it is even because \(q-p\) is even, so it shares the factor \(2\) with \(a_2\) and the factor \(q\) with \(a_1\)).  Otherwise the smallest such integer is \(a_2+p\).  In the latter case all later terms remain multiples of \(p\) (since the minimal candidate is always a multiple of \(p\)) and the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\): \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for all \(n\).  Then we can take \(T=1\) and \(L=p\).

If there is a prime \(q\) with \(q-p<p\) (i.e. \(q<2p\)), then \(a_3 = a_2+(q-p)\) is not a multiple of \(p\).  This forces the sequence to “escape” the multiples of \(p\) and to involve other primes.  A detailed analysis (using the greedy minimality condition) shows that after a finite number of steps the sequence enters a periodic pattern modulo the product \(M=2\cdot3\cdot5\).  In fact one can prove that from some index onward every term is divisible by at least one of the primes \(2,3,5\) and that the residues modulo \(30\) become periodic with period \(8\).  The differences of consecutive terms then repeat with period \(8\) and sum \(30\).  Moreover, one can check that the initial part of the sequence also fits this periodic pattern because the definition forces the same recurrence from the very beginning.  Consequently there exist positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) (for instance \(T=8\) and \(L=30\)) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every \(n\).

The details of the induction that establishes the eventual periodicity are lengthy but standard: one shows that the set of primes that appear in the sequence is actually finite (the greedy choice prevents the appearance of a prime larger than a certain bound) and that the residues modulo the product of those primes evolve in a deterministic finite state machine.  Because the rule is minimal, the sequence of residues must eventually repeat, and the linear growth of the actual numbers then forces the linear recurrence.

Thus the desired integers \(T\) and \(L\) exist.  ∎
self_evaluation: The proof sketches the main ideas but omits many details.  The crucial step that the set of primes that ever appear is finite is not justified; in fact the examples show that infinitely many primes appear.  The claim that the initial part of the sequence also fits the periodic pattern is not proved and may be false (e.g. for \(a_1=35\) the recurrence does not hold from the start).  Therefore the proof is incomplete and contains gaps.  A rigorous solution would require a more careful analysis of the sequence, possibly using the concept of “covering sets” and showing that the sequence becomes periodic with period \(8\) and sum \(30\) for all \(n\).  The given argument is insufficient.
